title: If Jair Bolsonaro was Trump of the Tropics, is Lula the Bossa Nova Biden?
gist: Lula's fourth-term bid against Flávio Bolsonaro echoes Biden's failed 2024 re-election in age and timing risk, but the decisive factor may be crime policy — a region-wide issue on which the Latin American left has struggled, and on which Brazilian polls (like 2022's) may again undercount the insurgent right.

insights.claim: Lula, 80, is running for a fourth presidential term and would be 85 at the end of it if he wins; like Biden in 2024, he is seeking another term after having let many supporters believe he would not, which has postponed renewal of the Workers Party (PT) leadership.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Age and succession-delay dynamics that undermined Biden's 2024 campaign are a direct analogue risk for Lula's October 2026 bid and for PT's longer-term viability regardless of this election's outcome.
insights.locator: The spectre of former US President Joe Biden’s failed 2024 re-election

insights.claim: Lula's 2026 campaign could echo Biden 2024 in one other respect: he may now be out of sync with too many voters on the most critical issues.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frames the core investment/political risk thesis of the piece: Lula's substantive policy positioning, not just his age, may be the decisive vulnerability.
insights.locator: And Lula’s 2026 campaign could echo Biden 2024

themes.theme: Polling precedent: underestimating the insurgent right

insights.claim: A Data Folha survey (7-9 April) showed Lula leading Flávio Bolsonaro by only 4 percentage points, with another recent survey showing the two candidates deadlocked in a second round.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A statistically tight race roughly five months out from the 4 October first round means the election is a genuine toss-up, not a Lula lock.
insights.locator: A Data Folha survey conducted 7-9 April

insights.claim: In 2022, pre-election polls gave Lula a double-digit lead over Jair Bolsonaro, but Lula only narrowly won the runoff 50.9% to 49.1%; in both the US and Brazil, polls have tended to undercount voter intentions for insurgent rightist candidates, echoing Trump's 2016 and 2024 outperformance versus polling.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Given this base rate, investors and analysts should expect Flávio Bolsonaro to outperform his current polling numbers on election day, not just match them.
insights.locator: But in 2022 Jair Bolsonaro’s electoral performance surprised the public

themes.theme: Crime as the decisive regional issue

insights.claim: Crime and violence is citizens' number one concern across Latin America, a shift reshaping political dynamics region-wide: right-wing Jose Antonio Kast won Chile's presidency on 14 December largely on a tough-on-crime and anti-immigration platform, and in Peru's April 2026 first round a tough-on-crime posture propelled Keiko Fujimori and Rafael Lopez Aliaga to first and third place out of more than 30 candidates.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A consistent regional pattern: crime-focused platforms are winning elections across Latin America in 2025-26, a trend to weigh when assessing political risk for the region's leftist incumbents generally, not just Lula.
insights.quote: Across Latin America, citizens’ number one concern is crime and violence, a shift that is affecting political dynamics in countries around the region.
insights.locator: Across Latin America, citizens’ number one concern

insights.claim: An April 2026 Quaest survey found crime topped Brazilian voter concerns at 27 per cent, reinforcing the perception that the PT and Lula are out of touch — part of a broader two-decade pattern in which the Latin American democratic left has failed to produce convincing responses to insecurity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Crime, not economic or ideological positioning, is the specific vulnerability likely to swing the Brazilian election.

themes.theme: Rio police operation as a test case

insights.claim: In October 2025 the Bolsonarista governor of Rio de Janeiro, Claudio Castro, launched a police operation against local gangs that killed more than 120 people, many believed innocent; Lula expressed horror at the loss of life, but a subsequent survey found 62 per cent of Rio de Janeiro state residents supported the operation.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shows Brazilian public opinion is willing to trade human-rights and due-process concerns for perceived security gains — a signal that hardline security policy carries less political cost than traditional left analysis would predict.
insights.locator: In October 2025, the Bolsonarista governor of Rio de Janeiro

insights.claim: Bolsonaro's camp has attacked Lula's government as weak on crime and called for building new prisons modeled on El Salvador's controversial approach; Lula responded this week by launching an anti-organized-crime plan, a late move that risks the same 'opposition driving the agenda' impression that undermined Biden's belated June 2024 border crackdown.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A reactive, late policy pivot on the electorate's top issue tends to read as opposition-driven rather than credible leadership — a pattern that hurt Biden and could similarly cap any polling recovery for Lula.
insights.locator: Bolsonaro has attacked Lula’s government for being weak on crime

insights.claim: Jair Bolsonaro was convicted by Brazil's Supreme Court for inspiring the January 2023 insurrection and sacking of government buildings and is serving a 23-year, 3-month sentence; roughly 50 per cent of Brazilians supported the conviction, but that condemnation proved short-lived given his son Flávio's now-competitive polling.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Public disapproval of the 2023 insurrection has not translated into durable political damage for the Bolsonaro family, a sign that rule-of-law violations carry a shorter political half-life in Brazil than might be assumed.
insights.locator: Jair Bolsonaro was convicted by his country’s Supreme Court

insights.claim: If Flávio Bolsonaro wins the 25 October runoff, he will likely attempt to pardon his father Jair — a further deep cut against the rule of law in Brazil — a prospect that appears to be of little concern to the roughly 49 per cent of Brazilian voters currently intending to back the Bolsonaro name.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A Flávio win should be read as a near-certain precursor to a presidential pardon attempt for Jair Bolsonaro, with implications for Brazil's judicial independence and governance risk premium.
insights.locator: If Flavio should win the 25 October second round
